title: LCPA — Projects
section: Final Projects for Laboratory of Computational Physics
text:
Final Projects for Laboratory of Computational Physics
In each of the branches of this repo you find all the necessary to complete your final project. In particular the file Project.ipynb describes the projects and provides guidance to its development. Other files could be present if needed.
Each branch is named after the group of students a given project is assigned to. The groups compositions are listed here
Students are supposed to work together to produce a short report on the assigned task. The preferred format for the latter is a jupyter notebook, with the proper description, the code implemented for the purpose and the actual results (plots, tables, etc.). The notebook has to be delivered with all the cells executed and should live in a GitHib repository. There is no need to make a pull request to the central repository.

Computing Resources
A Virtual Machine within CloudVeneto can be created for each group. Note that, by default, they are not. For some projects though, large datasets are needed, in those cases a VM has been (are being) created to store those files. Refer to ClouldInstructions.md for the steps to take in order to use those resources.
Alternatively, students can use colab (for which though no instructions are provided here).

title: LCPA — Projects
section: Group 17 - Final Project: Hierarchical mergers of binary black holes
text:
Group 17 - Final Project: Hierarchical mergers of binary black holes
This repository is a focused fork of the LCP Projects Y8 Central Repo. We are working exclusively on the Group17 branch to complete our final project.

Setup & Dependencies
We use uv for dependency management to ensure everyone has the same environment.

Installing uv
If uv is not already installed, install it with pip (recommended):
python -m pip install --user --upgrade uv
# or system-wide
python -m pip install --upgrade uv
This provides the uv CLI used below.


1. Clone & Isolation

With SSH key
Clone only our branch to keep the environment clean:
git clone --branch Group17 --single-branch git@github.com:PhyAMR/LCP_projects_Y8.git
cd LCP_projects_Y8


Without SSH key
Clone only the Group17 branch
git clone --branch Group17 --single-branch https://github.com/PhyAMR/LCP_projects_Y8.git
cd LCP_projects_Y8



2. Configure Remotes
Connect to the original source to pull core updates:
git remote add upstream git@github.com:PhysicsOfData/LCP_projects_Y8.git
git remote set-branches origin Group17
git fetch --prune origin


3. Environment Setup
Install the required libraries (Python version and dependencies) automatically:
uv sync  # reads pyproject.toml / uv.lock and creates a .venv for the project
This creates a .venv folder. Use uv run jupyter notebook or set your IDE to use the interpreter inside .venv.




Collaboration Workflow (Rebase Strategy)
To avoid a “spiderweb” of merge commits and keep our history linear, we use the Pull-Rebase-Push cycle.

Before you start: Sync with your teammates.

git pull --rebase origin Group17

Work and Commit: Keep changes small.

git add .
git commit -m "Brief description of changes"

Sync before pushing: In case someone pushed while you were working.

git fetch origin
git rebase origin/Group17

Push:

git push origin Group17



⚠️ Troubleshooting

Conflict Resolution
If git rebase stops due to a conflict:

Open the files and find the <<<<<<< HEAD markers.
Resolve the conflict manually.
Run git add <file> (do NOT commit).
Run git rebase --continue.

title: MAPDB — Projects
text:
QUAX Streaming Data Processing Pipeline

Overview
This project implements a quasi real-time streaming pipeline for processing data from the QUAX experiment. The main goal is to build a system capable of:

Processing detector data in near real-time
Monitoring detector performance live
Handling large-scale continuous data streams efficiently

The system is designed as a streaming ETL pipeline using Kafka + distributed processing (Dask or PySpark) + live visualization tools.



Scientific Context
The QUAX experiment searches for axions, hypothetical particles proposed to solve the strong CP problem in Quantum Chromodynamics (QCD). Axions are also a potential dark matter candidate.
Astrophysical constraints suggest an axion mass range:

( 1 ,eV < m < 10 , meV )


Detection Principle
QUAX uses:

A copper cavity
A static magnetic field (8.1 T)
Cryogenic temperatures (~150 mK)

Axions may couple to photons and produce a weak radio-frequency signal, amplified using a Josephson parametric amplifier.




Data Acquisition
The experiment generates two synchronized data streams:

duck_i → In-phase component (I)
duck_q → Quadrature component (Q)

Each sample:

32-bit float (little-endian)
ADC sampling rate: 2 MS/s
Data throughput: 128 Mbps (~16 MB/s)

Each file contains:

( 2^{23} ) samples
Duration per file pair: ~4.2 seconds

A full run produces thousands of scans over long periods (hours to weeks).



Data Structure
Files are stored in a CloudVeneto S3 bucket:

Bucket: quax
Access: Cloud object storage (Swift-compatible)

Each dataset consists of:

duck_i.dat
duck_q.dat

Each pair represents a continuous acquisition segment.



Data Processing Pipeline
The processing consists of two main steps:

1. FFT per Scan
Each scan is transformed from time domain to frequency domain using:

FFT size: ( n_{bins} = 2^{11} = 2048 )
Full bandwidth: -1 MHz to 1 MHz

Each file produces:

~4096 FFTs per batch



2. Averaging
For each batch:

Compute FFT for each scan
Average all FFTs
Compute standard deviation per frequency bin

Output:

Averaged power spectrum
Error estimates per bin





Streaming Architecture
The system is built as a Kafka-based pipeline:
Kafka Producer → Kafka Topic (raw data) → Dask/PySpark → Kafka Topic (processed data) → Consumer Dashboard

Components

1. Data Emulator (Producer)

Reads stored .dat files
Simulates real-time acquisition
Emits file pairs every ~5 seconds (configurable)



2. Stream Processing Layer
Implemented with:

Dask (preferred) or PySpark

Responsibilities:

Decode binary files
Split into scans
Perform FFT in parallel
Compute averaged spectrum
Generate summary statistics

Output format (example):
{
  "average": {
    "frequency": [...],
    "value": [...],
    "rms": [...]
  }
}

Send results to Kafka topic




3. Live Monitoring Dashboard
A consumer application:

Reads processed Kafka topic
Updates plots every ~5 seconds
Displays:

Current batch spectrum
Error bars (RMS)
Optional cumulative spectrum


Recommended tools:

matplotlib / pyplot
Bokeh (for interactive dashboard)






System Goals

Scientific Goals

Detect potential axion-induced spectral excess
Monitor detector stability in real time
Identify anomalies during long acquisition runs



Engineering Goals

Build a scalable streaming ETL system
Ensure near real-time processing
Handle high-throughput scientific data





Why Streaming?
This approach is necessary because:

Data is produced continuously at high rate
Storage on DAQ servers is limited
Long runs require live validation
Early anomaly detection is critical




Development Strategy
Recommended workflow:

Implement processing for a single batch (static)
Extend to batch processing with Dask/PySpark
Add Kafka streaming layer
Build live dashboard consumer
Optimize throughput and latency




Hints

Start simple before scaling to streaming
Use low-level APIs (Dask Bags / RDDs) initially if needed
Ensure efficient binary parsing (struct.unpack)
Design Kafka messages carefully (avoid oversized payloads)
Keep pipeline modular for debugging




Final Architecture Summary

Producer → simulates DAQ stream
Kafka topic (raw) → buffers file pairs
Distributed processing layer → FFT + averaging
Kafka topic (processed) → results stream
Consumer dashboard → real-time visualization

flowchart LR

%% =====================
%% DATA GENERATION
%% =====================
A[QUAX DAQ Archive\n(.dat files: duck_i / duck_q)] --> B[Stream Emulator Producer]

B -->|Kafka topic: raw_stream| C[(Kafka Broker\nraw data topic)]

%% =====================
%% PROCESSING LAYER
%% =====================
C --> D[Dask / PySpark Cluster]

D --> D1[Binary Parser\n(struct unpack)]
D1 --> D2[Scan Segmentation]
D2 --> D3[FFT per scan\n(2048 bins)]
D3 --> D4[Batch Averaging\n+ RMS computation]

D4 -->|processed spectra| E[(Kafka Broker\nresults topic)]

%% =====================
%% CONSUMPTION LAYER
%% =====================
E --> F[Live Consumer Service]

F --> F1[Real-time Plotter\nMatplotlib / Bokeh]
F1 --> G[Live Dashboard\nDetector Monitoring]

%% =====================
%% OPTIONAL STORAGE
%% =====================
E --> H[(Optional Storage\nHDFS / S3 / Filesystem)]

title: LCPB — Projects
text:
Grassi/

Machine Learning Project
This project uses uv for environment management.

Directory Structure and Organization Guidelines

config.yaml: The master configuration file. All hyperparameters, file paths, and general settings should live here. Avoid hardcoding values in scripts.
main.py: The single entry point to run the pipeline. It orchestrates the flow from data loading to training and evaluation.


data/
Contains all datasets. Note: Large data files should be added to .gitignore. - raw/: The immutable, original data (like your initial training set). Never modify scripts to overwrite these files. - processed/: Cleaned and transformed data ready for modeling. Should always be reproducible from the raw data via your scripts.


src/
Contains all source code for the project. Keep logic modular here. - __init__.py: Makes the src directory a Python package. - data_loader.py: Handles data ingestion, cleaning, and preprocessing. - preprocessing.py: Additional preprocessing steps. - models.py: Defines neural network architectures and other model classes. - trainer.py: Contains training/validation loops and handles model checkpointing. - analyzer.py: Generates evaluation metrics, plots, and reports. - plots.py: Contains functions for creating various types of plots. - cylinder.py: Helper script for cylinder-based geometries or related logic. - models/: Contains individual scripts/modules per model used: - cnn_model.py: Convolutional Neural Network formulation. - graphnn.py: Graph Neural Network formulation.


test/
Contains test scripts and exploratory code runs. - load_raw.py: Script to explore loading raw data.


outputs/
Stores all generated artifacts from script executions. These folders should generally be ignored by version control. - weights/: Trained model files (e.g., .pt, .pth, .safetensors). - plots/: Visualizations like loss curves, sample predictions, and confusion matrices. - reports/: Evaluation summaries, performance tables, and CSVs.

title: ASPA — Projects
section: Group Project for Advanced Statistics for Physical Analysis
text:
Group Project for Advanced Statistics for Physical Analysis

Modeling Anthropogenic CO2 Accumulation: A Bayesian Comparison of Linear and Accelerating Trends


Git & GitHub Collaboration Workflow
Since we are three people working on this project, we have organized the repository so that each person edits their own specific files: * Code: Each person has their own files in the code/ folder (e.g., code/EDA/, code/Inference/, code/Prediction/). * Report: Each person writes their portion of the report in their own text file within report/texts/ (e.g., ALVARO.md, CARLOS.md, etc.).
By always working in separate files, we can mostly avoid complex merge conflicts. However, it’s very important to keep your local repository in sync with the GitHub repository.

Standard Workflow (Every time you work)
Follow these steps every time you sit down to work on the project to ensure everything goes smoothly:

1. Pull the latest changes
Always pull before you start working so you have your teammates’ latest updates:
git checkout main
git pull origin main


2. Work on your files
Edit your specific files in code/*/ or report/texts/. Save your changes.


3. Check your status
See which files you have modified:
git status


4. Stage your changes
Add the specific files you worked on. (Try to avoid git add . unless you are sure you only touched your files).
# Example: Adding your code and report files
git add code/EDA/test.R
git add report/texts/ALVARO.md


5. Commit your changes
Write a short, descriptive message about what you did:
git commit -m "Update EDA analysis and add draft to report"


6. Pull and Push
Before pushing, it’s a good practice to pull one more time in case someone else pushed changes while you were working.
# Fetch and merge any new changes 
git pull origin main

# Push your changes to GitHub
git push origin main



Dealing with Merge Conflicts
Since we are working on separate files, conflicts should be rare. However, if you and a teammate accidentally edit the same file (like the main.pdf or this README.md) at the same time, Git will warn you about a conflict when you pull.
If that happens: 1. Open the conflicted file. Git will mark the conflict with <<<<<<<, =======, and >>>>>>>. 2. Edit the file to keep what you need, and delete those markers. 3. Save the file. 4. Add and commit the resolved file: bash    git add <conflicted_file>    git commit -m "Resolve merge conflict in <file>"    git push origin main

title: PCN — Projects
text:
theoretical_projects/topic_01_statistical_mechanics_on_complex_networks/

Topic 1. Statistical mechanics on complex networks
The objective of this project is to simulate the behavior of some well-known models, and possibly analyze their critical behavior (i.e., how the order parameter changes with the control parameter). The students are left free to follow the bibliographic references to understand what should be simulated and how, as well as to reproduce the main results and, if willing to do so, explore new directions. It is required that these models are studied, computationally, across a variety of different synthetic topologies. Applications to real-world networks will be a plus.

title: PCN — Projects
text:
theoretical_projects/topic_02_growth_models_with_structural_phase_transitions_i/

Topic 2. Growth models with structural phase transitions I
The objective of this project is to simulate the behavior of some well-known growth models, and possibly analyze their critical behavior (e.g., the emergence of a giant connected component for varying control parameter(s) of each model). The students are left free to follow the bibliographic references to understand what should be simulated and how, as well as to reproduce the main results and, if willing to do so, explore new directions.

The students are left free to follow the bibliographic references to understand what should be simulated and how, as well as to reproduce the main results and, if willing to do so, explore new directions.

theoretical_projects/topic_04_growth_models_with_structural_phase_transitions_iii/

The students are left free to follow the bibliographic references to understand what should be simulated and how, as well as to reproduce the main results and, if willing to do so, explore new directions.

title: PCN — Projects
text:
theoretical_projects/topic_05_cascade_failures/

Topic 5. Cascade failures
The objective of this project is to simulate the behavior of some well-known dynamical models, and possibly analyze their critical behavior (e.g., the breakdown of the largest connected component for varying control parameter(s) of each model). The students are left free to follow the bibliographic references to understand what should be simulated and how, as well as to reproduce the main results and, if willing to do so, explore new directions.

title: PCN — Projects
text:
theoretical_projects/topic_06_dynamics_on_networks/

Topic 6. Dynamics on networks
The objective of this project is to simulate the behavior of some well-known dynamical models, and possibly analyze their behavior (e.g., their parameter space or their dependence on targeted perturbations). The students are left free to follow the bibliographic references to understand what should be simulated and how, as well as to reproduce the main results and, if willing to do so, explore new directions.

title: PCN — Projects
text:
theoretical_projects/topic_07_dynamics_on_networks_ii/

Topic 7. Dynamics on networks II
The objective of this project is to simulate the behavior of some well-known dynamical models, and possibly analyze their behavior (e.g., identify the critical epidemic threshold or the invasion threshold). The students are left free to follow the bibliographic references to understand what should be simulated and how, as well as to reproduce the main results and, if willing to do so, explore new directions.

title: PCN — Projects
text:
theoretical_projects/topic_08_quantum_networks/

Topic 8. Quantum networks
The objective of this project is to simulate the behavior of some known dynamical models for quantum networks. The students are left free to follow the bibliographic references to understand what should be simulated and how, as well as to reproduce the main results and, if willing to do so, explore new directions.

title: PCN — Projects
text:
theoretical_projects/topic_09_sociophysics_i/

Topic 9. Sociophysics I
The objective of this project is to simulate some well known dynamical models of Sociophysics, and possibly analyze their behavior (e.g., the influence of the dynamical parameters and network topological traits on the behavior of the model). For the voter model, it is enough to simulate and understand its classical version and see how the properties change when variations in the update rules are considered. The students are left free to follow the bibliographic references to understand what should be simulated and how, as well as to reproduce the main results and, if willing to do so, explore new directions.

title: PCN — Projects
text:
theoretical_projects/topic_10_sociophysics_ii/

Topic 10. Sociophysics II
The objective of this project is to simulate some well known dynamical models of Sociophysics, and possibly analyze their behavior (e.g., the influence of the dynamical parameters and network topological traits on the behavior of the model). The students are left free to follow the bibliographic references to understand what should be simulated and how, as well as to reproduce the main results and, if willing to do so, explore new directions.

title: PCN — Projects
text:
theoretical_projects/topic_11_sociophysics_iii/

Topic 11. Sociophysics III
The objective of this project is to simulate some well known dynamical models of Sociophysics, and possibly analyze their behavior (e.g., the influence of the dynamical parameters and network topological traits on the behavior of the model). The students are left free to follow the bibliographic references to understand what should be simulated and how, as well as to reproduce the main results and, if willing to do so, explore new directions.

title: PCN — Projects
text:
theoretical_projects/topic_12_percolation/

Topic 12. Percolation
The objective of this project is to simulate the percolation process for different removing protocols, and characterize the phase space and the critical properties (e.g., the size of the giant component, the distribution of small clusters, assessing whether the transition of first/second/hybrid order, etc.) The students are left free to follow the bibliographic references to understand what should be simulated and how, as well as to reproduce the main results and, if willing to do so, explore new directions.
